author: @Gwydion67
text:
Hi Robert, 
thanks for sharing! 😃
In fact, today I learned one thing:
It's not self-evident that everyone else is looking at the topic with that understanding ("generators do not CREATE energy, they CONVERT the already existing").
You do, I do, maybe many fellow subscribers do. But not the "average guy".
Good to realize when talking to people next time. 👍

author: @al3xf103
text: Hi Robert, love your videos. Regarding this one, I was under the seemingly mistaken impression that not all ‘converters’ are created equal, and that something like Christopher Laimer’s 3D printed halbach array motor would be the best kind of generator, especially for a wind turbine. But after watching your video and re-reading Wikipedia, it seems bldc’s are just as efficient if not more than that particular design. The only advantage of the 3D printed one is that you can 3D print a motor/generator to the spec you so desire if you wish to. But grabbing something from inside a blender is not only easier, cheaper, takes less time but is also just as efficient??🤔

author: @William_Hada
text: Yes, exactly,  but do you realize that you have spoiled some people's hobby of trying to create energy from nothing? Joy killer! :)

author: @make425
text:
Well if you don't have a correct base voltage from x rpm on the creation. You'll never make a proper input creation  With out waisting

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @make425
replies.text:
My point is that "I" believe that the capture "input" should be designed more around the creation "generator".  
The more blades or more surface area the lower the wind speed needed to turn.✔️
Then you have blade length or diameter. Your power needed to turn the generator.✔️
So if you're basing your blades strictly on wind speed.
And not adding the needed power to even turn the generator.
Idk 😶
Chicken or the 🥚.
Who cares put it in a pan and enjoy it right ☺️

The gears and stuff are the biggest losses.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @morski_kapetan
text: "output is always lower than the input". What does 'always' mean?

replies.author: @MrDohr83
replies.text: You always have any kind of loss. With motors and generators there are thermal loss, iron loss and copper loss.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: are you a native English speaker?

replies.author: @morski_kapetan
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering nope :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @morski_kapetan cool - in these terms, mathematically A > B for all cases, where A = input, B= Output

author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
text: 90% of the time people making diy wind turbines ignore gearing.. which happens to be key to achieve motor rated RPM at average wind speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely agreed mate

author: @bobvincent5921
text: Thanks. Sure the overall efficiency from turbine to generator to storage method is on the lines of 0.5 × 0.7 × 0.8 = 28℅, and the input is free of charge, I for one will make my hot water this way

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Robert,
it is for me cristal clear that I cannot create energy: It's always a convertion  as here wind to mecanical and mecanical to electric energy. 
To create a wind turbine is a very complex chapter - as you shown in your videos. I prefered the vertical variante, but your "Wind Rose" made change my mind: Easy to create, not very much vibration and low noise / intra sound. Next was your statement that no need to variate the horizontal system in every possible wind direction: Just keep it in the main wind direction. (And build one more static direction than take the complex mecanical adventute with variable direction) That's what I was learning in your videos. Thanks for that!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: bless your heart mate and cheers
